ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic chutney class; no tamarind-specific chutney entry exists. Mapped to closest parent class FOODON_03311737 (chutney food product).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a tamarind-chutney-specific code; A0CKP (Chutneys and relishes) is the closest defensible parent node.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for tamarind chutney identified in clinical terminology; not applicable for this condiment record.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of tamarind chutney is approximately 30 g (about 2 tablespoons), drizzled over chaat or used as a dipping sauce for samosas and pakoras.

nutrient_source.title: Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 (IFCT 2017)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for tamarind chutney are adapted from ICMR-NIN IFCT 2017 data for tamarind pulp and jaggery, cross-referenced with the input enriched_json values (confidence 0.9). No exact tamarind chutney composite entry exists in IFCT 2017; values represent a defensible estimate for the cooked, sweetened preparation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and water content are not reported in the available source for this preparation and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Tamarind-based and Jaggery-sweetened Condiments — analogous estimate based on tamarind pulp and jaggery GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for tamarind chutney exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 62 (medium) is estimated from the GI of tamarind pulp (~65) and jaggery (~84), weighted by their dominant contribution to total carbohydrate. The high dietary fibre (3.8 g/100 g) and organic acids in tamarind are expected to moderately attenuate the glycemic response. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (62 × 54 × 0.30) / 100 ≈ 10.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 380 mg per 100 g; typical 30 g serving contributes ~114 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit tamarind chutney to small portions (≤15 g per serving) and choose preparations made without added black salt or table salt to keep sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high simple sugar content (48.5 g sugar per 100 g) predominantly from jaggery; medium GI of 62 with high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should use tamarind chutney sparingly (≤10 g per occasion) or substitute with a sugar-free tamarind preparation, as the high jaggery-derived sugar content will cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.3 g/100 g) and no cholesterol, but high sugar content and moderate sodium are relevant for cardiovascular risk management
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While tamarind chutney is virtually fat-free and cholesterol-free, its high sugar content (48.5 g/100 g) and moderate sodium (380 mg/100 g) mean that individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict portion size to ≤15 g and prefer low-sugar formulations to avoid contributing to dyslipidaemia and hypertension.

acid_reflux.key_factor: High organic acid content from tamarind pulp
acid_reflux.guidance: Individuals with acid reflux or GERD should avoid tamarind chutney, as the high acidity of tamarind can exacerbate symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tamarind Chutney (also known as Imli ki Chutney or Saunth) is a thick, syrupy, dark-brown Indian condiment made by cooking tamarind pulp with jaggery (unrefined cane sugar) and a blend of ground spices including dry ginger powder (saunth), roasted cumin, red chili, and black salt. The preparation involves boiling and reducing the tamarind pulp with sweetener until a glossy, glaze-like consistency is achieved. The flavour profile is a complex interplay of sharp sourness from tamarind, deep caramel-like sweetness from jaggery, earthy warmth from cumin and ginger, and a subtle heat from chili. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 215 kcal, 54 g carbohydrate (of which 48.5 g is sugar), 1.5 g protein, and only 0.3 g fat, making it a high-sugar, low-fat condiment. It contains 3.8 g dietary fibre and 380 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 62 (medium), but the high sugar load means even a 30 g serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 10 g, making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in regular or large quantities. Tamarind chutney is a cornerstone of Indian street food culture, used as the essential sweet-sour drizzle on chaat dishes such as Bhel Puri, Papdi Chaat, Dahi Vada, and Samosa Chaat, and as a dipping sauce for samosas and pakoras. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: What is tamarind chutney made of?
How many calories in tamarind chutney?

dipping sauce for samosas, pakoras, and kachori
accompaniment for Indian street food snacks
condiment at snack time or as part of a chaat platter

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN IFCT 2017 data for tamarind pulp and jaggery components; no exact composite tamarind chutney entry exists in the approved source database. Input enriched_json values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with expected composition for this preparation and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): tamarind chutney is a plant-based condiment with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero per ICMR-NIN IFCT 2017 plant food entries. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no UV-exposed animal or fungal ingredients are present; vitamin D is naturally zero for this preparation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for tamarind chutney are not reported in ICMR-NIN IFCT 2017 or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation. 3. saturated_fat_g of 0.1 g is estimated from the negligible fat content (0.3 g total fat) consistent with tamarind pulp and jaggery composition in ICMR-NIN IFCT 2017. 4. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate derived from University of Sydney GI Database values for tamarind pulp and jaggery; no direct measurement for tamarind chutney exists. 5. Portions in the input contained four identical duplicate entries (30 g each); deduplicated to a single 30 g serving. 6. The input source 'Journal of Pharmacognosy and Phytochemistry' is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence for nutrient values; it is retained only as contextual background for health suitability narrative.
